data.title: Provident Financial Earnings Call: Margin Tailwinds, Growth Risks
data.description:
Provident Financial Holdings ((PROV)) has held its Q2 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Provident Financial Holdings Sees Solid Core Trends Amid Loan Payoff Headwinds

Provident Financial Holdings’ latest earnings call painted a picture of a fundamentally sound franchise with improving core trends in lending, credit quality, and funding costs, but also highlighted meaningful headwinds from elevated loan payoffs and some future repricing uncertainty. Management emphasized stronger loan origination activity, healthier asset quality metrics, and a modestly higher net interest margin supported by easing funding costs and robust capital levels. However, accelerated loan payoffs trimmed loan balances and weighed on margin, operating expenses ticked up due in part to a one-time settlement, and upcoming loan repricing in 2026 could challenge growth if market rates move unfavorably. Overall, the tone was cautiously optimistic: operationally solid with visible levers for margin expansion, though near-term balance sheet growth remains dependent on payoff and repricing dynamics.

Loan Originations Accelerate but Fail to Overcome Payoffs

Provident posted a strong rebound in lending activity, with loans held for investment originations reaching $42.1 million in the quarter, a 42% increase from $29.6 million in the prior period. Management indicated that loan pipelines are moderately higher, suggesting March quarter originations should again fall within the recent $28 million–$42 million range. Yet this momentum was more than offset by loan principal payments and payoffs of $46.7 million, up 35% from $34.5 million in the prior quarter. As a result, total loans held for investment declined by about $4.1 million despite the stronger production. The call underscored that elevated payoffs are currently a key brake on loan growth and a swing factor for near‑term balance sheet expansion.

Credit Quality Improves Further, Supporting a Low-Risk Profile

Asset quality was a clear bright spot. Nonperforming assets fell to $990,000, or just 0.08% of total assets, down from $1.9 million the prior quarter, and management reported no loans in early-stage delinquency. The company recorded a $158,000 recovery of credit losses, reflecting ongoing strength in portfolio performance. The allowance for credit losses stood at 55 basis points of gross loans, only slightly lower than 56 basis points previously, suggesting no aggressive reserve drawdown. While certain loan categories—including multifamily, commercial business and commercial real estate—declined, increases in single‑family and construction loans came without deterioration in credit metrics. Overall, Provident’s credit profile appears conservatively managed, with limited immediate stress noted in the portfolio.

Margin Edges Higher as Funding Costs Ease

Provident reported a modest but meaningful improvement in profitability drivers, as net interest margin (NIM) increased by 3 basis points to 3.03%. This was aided by a decline in the average cost of deposits to 1.32%, down 2 basis points, and a 20‑basis‑point reduction in the cost of borrowing to 4.39%. These funding cost improvements, combined with better loan yields, support the case for ongoing margin expansion. However, the quarter’s NIM was also pressured by net deferred loan cost amortization tied to heightened loan payoffs, which reduced NIM by approximately 5 basis points versus no impact in the prior quarter. Without this drag, underlying margin trends would have looked even stronger, reinforcing management’s view that funding and pricing levers can drive further NIM gains.

New Loan Production Carrying Higher Yields Than Legacy Portfolio

The bank is successfully originating higher‑yielding assets relative to its existing book. Loans originated in the quarter carried a weighted average rate of 6.15%, well above the 5.22% weighted average rate of loans currently held for investment. This positive spread implies that as new production gradually replaces older, lower‑yielding loans, the loan portfolio’s overall yield should rise, providing a structural tailwind to NIM. The challenge, however, is timing: elevated prepayments and payoffs accelerate the replacement process but also bring forward the amortization of deferred costs, temporarily holding back reported margin. Over time, if originations remain healthy, the higher yields on new loans should outweigh these near‑term technical pressures.

Funding Repricing Offers a Clear Margin Upside Catalyst

Management highlighted significant upcoming opportunities to lower funding costs as wholesale borrowings reprice. Approximately $109 million of wholesale funding maturing in March 2026, with a weighted average rate of 4.12%, and $79.5 million maturing in June 2026 at 4.15% are expected to be refinanced at lower prevailing market rates. This represents a sizable portion of the funding stack that can potentially be reset at cheaper levels, directly benefiting NIM. Coupled with already declining deposit and borrowing costs, this repricing window is a key margin catalyst, particularly for the March 2026 timeframe, and one of the clearer levers for earnings improvement highlighted on the call.

Capital Strength Fuels Aggressive Shareholder Returns

Provident remains well‑capitalized by a comfortable margin, and management continues to lean into capital returns. The company maintained its cash dividend and repurchased approximately $1.5 million of common stock in the fiscal second quarter, in addition to about $96,000 of buybacks in the December quarter. Combined with $906,000 of cash dividends, capital distributions for the quarter represented roughly 170% of the December quarter’s net income. While this underscores confidence in the balance sheet and capital position, it may raise questions about sustainability if earnings were to soften, especially given the current loan growth and margin headwinds. For now, though, the strategy signals a shareholder‑friendly stance backed by robust capital buffers.

Efficiency Focus Amid Rising Operating Costs

The company continues to operate with a lean staffing model and a tight grip on expenses. Full‑time equivalent headcount stood at 163, essentially flat versus 162 a year ago, underscoring a cautious approach to adding personnel. Operating expenses rose to $7.9 million from $7.6 million in the prior quarter, largely driven by a $214,000 pre‑litigation voluntary mediation settlement related to an employment matter. Despite this one‑time cost, management reiterated an expected quarterly operating expense run rate of $7.6 million–$7.7 million for the remainder of fiscal 2026. The message to investors is that the bank intends to protect profitability through expense discipline even as it invests selectively to support loan growth.

Loan Mix Shifts as Certain Categories Contract

Loan balances showed a mix shift during the quarter. While overall loans held for investment declined, the reduction came primarily from multifamily, commercial business, and commercial real estate segments. These declines were partially offset by growth in single‑family and construction loans. The shift reflects both customer behavior and management’s portfolio positioning amid evolving market conditions. Importantly, the changes occurred against a backdrop of stable to improving credit quality, suggesting that Provident is managing concentration risks while still finding pockets of demand in residential and construction lending.

Loan Repricing in 2026 Introduces Growth and Payoff Risks

Looking ahead, loan repricing in 2026 represents a key uncertainty. About $125.2 million of loans are scheduled to reprice in June 2026 to an interest rate roughly 38 basis points higher, from 6.11% to 6.49%. If prevailing market rates at that time are meaningfully lower than these reset levels, some borrowers could seek to refinance elsewhere, potentially triggering additional payoffs and weighing on loan growth. Conversely, if market rates are similar or higher, the repricing could boost loan yields and support margin. The call acknowledged that this dynamic is a potential headwind for balance sheet expansion and a variable to watch as 2026 approaches.

Forward-Looking Outlook: Margin Upside vs. Growth Constraints

Management’s guidance suggests a cautiously constructive path forward. Loan origination momentum is expected to continue, with March quarter originations likely within the $28 million–$42 million range, though principal payments and payoffs remain a swing factor for net loan growth. Asset quality is expected to stay strong, with low nonperforming assets, no early delinquencies, and modest reserve coverage deemed adequate, while commercial real estate office exposure is limited and related maturities are small for the remainder of fiscal 2026. On the margin front, the bank sees upside as deposit and borrowing costs continue to drift lower, new loans come on at higher yields than the existing book, and substantial wholesale funding maturing in 2026 is refinanced at lower rates. Some offset is anticipated from accelerated deferred cost amortization associated with payoffs and from loan repricing in mid‑2026, which could either support yields or spur additional paydowns. Operating expenses are guided to a $7.6 million–$7.7 million quarterly run rate, and capital returns—through dividends and buybacks—are expected to continue, albeit their long‑term pace will likely depend on how earnings evolve as these various rate and payoff dynamics play out.

In summary, Provident Financial Holdings’ earnings call portrayed a bank with solid credit quality, improving margin drivers, and strong capital backing an active shareholder return program, but also one grappling with elevated loan payoffs and prospective repricing challenges that may cap near‑term growth. Investors following the stock will likely focus on whether robust higher‑yield origination and lower funding costs can outpace the drag from payoffs and loan mix shifts, and on how management adjusts capital return levels if earnings volatility increases. For now, the story is one of steady fundamentals with credible levers for profitability improvement, tempered by execution risk around balance sheet growth in a still‑evolving rate environment.

data.title: National Bank Holdings Corp Charts Bold Post-Vista Path
data.description:
National Bank Holdings Corp ((NBHC)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

National Bank Holdings Corp Strikes Optimistic Tone Despite Near-Term Cost Pressures

National Bank Holdings Corp’s latest earnings call struck a notably upbeat tone, with management emphasizing a fortress-like capital position, successful execution of the Vista Bank acquisition, and robust loan origination momentum. While the quarter was weighed down by meaningful one-time charges, elevated credit costs tied to a small group of problem loans, and a step-up in 2026 expense guidance, executives framed these as manageable and largely transitory headwinds. The strategic narrative centered on margin resilience around 4%, improving credit metrics, and the build-out of the 2UniFi digital platform, all underpinning an ambitious multi-year earnings target of more than $1 per share in the fourth quarter of 2026 and over $4 per share in 2027.

Capital Strength and Tangible Book Value Expansion

National Bank Holdings underscored a year of notable capital accretion, with tangible book value per share up 10% in 2025 and key regulatory ratios comfortably above peers. The Common Equity Tier 1 ratio finished at roughly 14.9%, while the tangible common equity ratio closed the year at 11% and Tier 1 leverage at 11.6%. These levels give the bank substantial flexibility to absorb integration costs from the Vista deal, fund organic growth, and support share repurchases, all while maintaining a conservative risk profile. Management repeatedly highlighted capital strength as a core competitive advantage heading into 2026.

Top-Quartile Net Interest Margin Holds Near 4%

Earnings power continues to be driven by one of the stronger net interest margins in the regional banking space. For 2025, National Bank Holdings delivered a full-year NIM of 3.94%, described as top quartile, with December’s spot margin rebounding to 3.97% and Vista’s December margin running near 4%. Management acknowledged some recent volatility from timing differences in loan and deposit repricing, but still expects the fully taxable-equivalent NIM to hover around 4% in 2026, assuming no major surprises from the Federal Reserve. That margin resilience, especially on a larger balance sheet post-Vista, is central to the company’s earnings growth story.

Record Commercial Originations Fuel Growth Pipeline

Loan growth was a standout theme, with the bank originating $1.6 billion in new loans during 2025. Fourth-quarter production reached $591 million, the second-highest quarter in the company’s history, and included a record $429 million in commercial loan originations. Commercial balances grew at nearly an 8% annualized pace in the quarter, underscoring healthy demand across the franchise. Management framed this momentum as evidence that the combined platform is well-positioned to capture quality growth and that the loan pipeline supports its roughly 10% loan growth target for 2026.

Vista Acquisition Rapidly Closed and Expands Scale

The acquisition of Vista Bank was a central strategic milestone, executed in under four months and adding roughly $2.4 billion of earning assets. The combined loan portfolio now stands near $9.4 billion, giving National Bank Holdings greater scale in its core markets and a broader base over which to spread costs. Management is targeting around 10% loan growth and 7%–10% growth in earning assets for 2026, using Vista’s contribution as a springboard rather than an endpoint. The transaction is being framed as clearly accretive over time, despite some near-term integration and rebranding expenses.

Credit Quality Trends Improve as Problem Loans Addressed

On the credit front, management pointed to tangible improvement in key metrics while acknowledging elevated provision expense in the quarter. The nonperforming asset ratio improved by 11 basis points in 2025 to 36 basis points of total loans, and criticized loans fell 73 basis points over the year. The allowance to total loans ratio sits near 1.18%, supplemented by $16.8 million of marks on acquired loans, which would add roughly 23 basis points of effective coverage if applied. By moving decisively on a handful of problem credits, the bank aims to enter 2026 with a cleaner book, even though this prudence pushed up fourth-quarter credit costs.

2UniFi Phase 1 Completion and New Product Launch

The bank also spotlighted its 2UniFi initiative, a digital and technology platform that completed Phase 1 during the year. This milestone enabled the launch of an automated SBA working capital loan product and enhanced business deposit capabilities, aiming to tap small-business demand with more scalable, tech-driven solutions. Management expects 2UniFi to contribute $2 million to $4 million of revenue in 2026 and noted that capital expenditures should start to decline as the build phase winds down. While still in early innings, 2UniFi is positioned as both a growth engine and a future efficiency lever.

Capital Return: New $100 Million Buyback Authorization

Shareholder returns featured prominently, with the board approving a $100 million share repurchase program. Management described buybacks as a capital deployment priority, even as the Vista deal has temporarily lifted the share count. The company projects roughly 45.8 million shares outstanding in 2026 after incorporating Vista-related issuance, and buybacks are intended to help offset this dilution over time. Combined with the strong capital ratios, the authorization signals confidence in the intrinsic value of the franchise and its long-term earnings trajectory.

Earnings Profile and Ambitious Multi-Year Outlook

Despite quarter-specific noise, underlying earnings performance was solid. Adjusted fourth-quarter net income, stripping out one-time items, came in at $22.7 million, or $0.60 per diluted share. For 2025 as a whole, adjusted net income reached $117.6 million, equating to $3.06 per diluted share. Management set a clear multi-year bar: the company aims to exceed $1.00 of EPS in the fourth quarter of 2026 and is positioning for more than $4.00 per share in 2027. These targets rely on sustained loan growth, stable margins around 4%, disciplined credit, and incremental contributions from 2UniFi and fee-based businesses.

Impact of One-Time Acquisition and Securities Losses

Headline results in the fourth quarter were weighed down by a pair of nonrecurring items. The company recorded $4.1 million of after-tax acquisition-related costs tied to the Vista transaction, alongside a $2.6 million after-tax loss on the sale of investment securities. Management explained that the securities sale was a strategic move designed to keep total assets below the $10 billion regulatory threshold for another year and thereby preserve roughly $10 million of interchange income. While the losses were material in the period, executives emphasized their one-time nature and the longer-term economic rationale.

Provision Expense, Charge-Offs, and a Cleaner Starting Point

Fourth-quarter provision expense totaled $9.1 million, driven primarily by charge-offs and specific reserves on a small number of problem loans. For 2025, net charge-offs ran at 34 basis points of average loans, higher than the bank’s typical range but largely concentrated in a limited set of exposures. Management portrayed these actions as a deliberate decision to “rip off the band-aid” and start 2026 with a cleaner credit slate, particularly important as the company integrates Vista and aims to scale earnings. The existing allowance level plus acquired loan marks are expected to be sufficient under current conditions, though a CECL day 1 adjustment remains possible once purchase accounting is finalized.

Higher Noninterest Expense and Steep 2026 Cost Guidance

Investors will need to look past a significant step-up in operating costs in the near term. Full-year noninterest expense for 2025 was $265 million, including $22 million related to 2UniFi. For 2026, management guided to noninterest expense of $320 million to $330 million, representing a roughly 21%–25% increase. The first half alone is expected to carry $165 million to $170 million in costs. The jump reflects a full year of Vista, technology investments, and anticipated one-time acquisition and rebranding charges. While clearly a headwind to near-term earnings, management framed these expenses as investments to support a larger, more profitable platform.

2UniFi Losses and Partnership Uncertainty

The 2UniFi platform remains a drag on reported earnings, at least for now. In 2025, 2UniFi generated about $22 million of expense, and management plans to hold that figure flat in 2026, even as projected revenue ranges only from $2 million to $4 million. Roughly half of next year’s 2UniFi expense will be depreciation. A potential partnership that could reduce the bank’s capital commitment and reshape the economics of 2UniFi is still being negotiated, and management cautioned that both timing and financial impact remain uncertain. Investors will be watching closely for tangible progress in monetizing this digital investment.

Securities Sale Caps Near-Term Balance Sheet Growth

The decision to sell securities and realize a loss to stay under the $10 billion asset threshold carries strategic implications beyond the quarter’s earnings drag. By remaining below that level, National Bank Holdings expects to preserve about $10 million in annual interchange income, which would otherwise be pressured under a different regulatory regime. The trade-off is that near-term balance sheet expansion is constrained, limiting how quickly the bank can deploy capital into earning assets. Management clearly judged the short-term pain worthwhile for the medium-term revenue benefit.

Margin Volatility from Repricing Dynamics

While the overall margin story is positive, management acknowledged some recent volatility driven by repricing dynamics. Variable-rate loans reset lower ahead of anticipated Federal Reserve cuts, while deposit costs have lagged, creating a temporary drag on NIM. Despite this mismatch, December’s spot margin improved to 3.97%, suggesting the worst of the near-term pressure may be passing. Executives stressed that they still expect the 2026 net interest margin to average around 4% on a fully taxable-equivalent basis, barring unexpected rate shocks.

Integration, CECL Day 1 Risk, and Acquisition-Driven Dilution

Looking ahead, the company cautioned about potential additional noise from the Vista integration. A CECL day 1 provision could be required depending on final purchase accounting, and management expects further one-time acquisition and rebranding expenses in 2026 that could weigh on reported earnings. The projected 2026 share count of about 45.8 million reflects Vista-related share issuance, introducing some dilution relative to prior levels. The newly authorized buyback is intended to partially offset this over time, but investors should expect near-term per-share metrics to reflect both the integration costs and the higher share base.

Forward-Looking Guidance and Earnings Trajectory

Guidance for 2026 sketched a detailed picture of the combined organization’s path forward. The bank expects starting loans of roughly $9.4 billion and aims for about 10% loan growth, with earning assets growing 7%–10%. Management anticipates a fully taxable-equivalent net interest margin around 4% and is targeting an asset mix with about 15% in cash and investments and a loan-to-deposit ratio near 90%. Noninterest income is projected at $75 million to $80 million, while total noninterest expense is guided to $320 million–$330 million, including $22 million tied to 2UniFi. Credit metrics are expected to remain solid, with allowance coverage near 1.2% plus incremental marks from acquired loans, supported by strong capital ratios including TCE of 11% and CET1 around 14.9%. The company also expects an effective tax rate near 20% and a 2026 share count of approximately 45.8 million, underpinning its goal of delivering more than $1.00 of EPS in the fourth quarter of 2026 and setting the stage for greater than $4.00 EPS in 2027.

In sum, National Bank Holdings Corp’s earnings call presented a story of a bank leaning into growth and technology investment from a position of strength, even as it absorbs elevated costs and integration-related noise in the near term. Strong capital, a resilient near-4% margin, improving credit quality, and record loan origination activity provide a solid foundation. Against that backdrop, investors will be weighing the temporary drag from one-time charges, higher operating expenses, and 2UniFi losses against management’s confident multi-year earnings roadmap and commitment to shareholder returns.

data.title: Badger Meter Earnings Call: Growth, Margins, and 2026 Setup
data.description:
Badger Meter ((BMI)) has held its Q4 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Badger Meter Showcases Record Growth but Flags 2026 Timing Bumps

Badger Meter’s latest earnings call struck a confident but measured tone. Management highlighted record revenue, expanding margins, and robust free cash flow, underscoring a strong multi‑year growth story and successful integration of recent acquisitions. At the same time, they were candid about near‑term headwinds: project pacing, cost pressures from tariffs and commodities, and the inherent timing uncertainty around large Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) deployments. The overarching message was clear: the long‑term outlook remains solid, even if quarterly results may be uneven.

Record Full-Year Revenue and Strong Top-Line Growth

Badger Meter crossed a major milestone in 2025, with revenue surpassing $900 million. That figure represents 11% year‑over‑year sales growth and a striking 17% compounded annual growth rate over the past five years. The sustained expansion underscores strong demand across its water technology portfolio and validates the company’s strategic focus on smart water metering and analytics. For investors, this multi‑year performance suggests that growth is not just cyclical but supported by structural trends in water infrastructure and digitalization.

Solid Q4 Sales Performance

Fourth quarter sales reached $221 million, up 8% from the prior year. Excluding the SmartCover acquisition, base growth was 2%, indicating that acquisitions and software are increasingly important contributors to the top line. Utility water product line sales were a particular bright spot, rising 9% year‑over‑year (2% on a base basis). While the quarter did not deliver break‑neck organic growth, it continued a steady upward trend and reflected resilient demand from water utilities.

Margin Expansion and Profitability Improvement

Profitability improved meaningfully in 2025. Full‑year operating profit margins expanded to 20.0%, an increase of 90 basis points from the prior year. In Q4, operating margins rose to 19.5%, up 40 basis points year‑over‑year, and base operating margins climbed even more to 20.5%, a 140‑basis‑point improvement. Gross margin in the fourth quarter improved to 42.1%, up 180 basis points year‑over‑year. These gains indicate that Badger Meter is managing pricing, mix, and costs effectively, even against a backdrop of inflationary input pressures.

Earnings and Cash Generation

Earnings growth kept pace with revenue and margin gains. Consolidated EPS for the fourth quarter came in at $1.14, up 10% from $1.04 a year earlier. Cash generation was particularly strong: the company delivered record quarterly free cash flow of $50.8 million, an increase of roughly $3.4 million year‑over‑year. For the full year, free cash flow conversion remained above 100% of net earnings, a key quality metric that signals disciplined working capital management and strong underlying cash economics.

Software Expansion and Rising Recurring Revenue

Software continues to grow as a strategic pillar of Badger Meter’s business. Software revenue, including SmartCover, exceeded $74 million in 2025 and now accounts for about 8% of total sales. Importantly, software has grown at a 28% compounded rate over the past five years. This shift matters for investors because software and analytics typically carry higher margins and a larger recurring revenue component, which can make cash flows more predictable and valuations more resilient over the long term.

SmartCover Integration Delivers Sales and Profit Upside

The integration of SmartCover appears to be progressing smoothly and ahead of expectations. SmartCover delivered approximately $40 million in sales in 2025, with about a quarter of that on an annualized basis, pointing to strong momentum. Management credited higher volumes and cost management for improved profitability, and noted that manufacturing has been transferred to Badger Meter’s Racine facility. The company reiterated that it expects SmartCover to be earnings‑accretive in 2026, reinforcing the strategic and financial benefits of the deal.

Major PRASA AMI Win Adds Multi-Year Growth Visibility

One of the headline developments is Badger Meter’s award from the Puerto Rico Aqueduct and Sewer Authority (PRASA), covering roughly 1.6 million service connections. This is one of the largest AMI deployments globally and represents a significant validation of Badger Meter’s technology. The company will serve as a supply‑only partner, providing meters, ORION Cellular AMI radios, and BEACON software‑as‑a‑service, with production centered in Racine. The project is expected to be deployed over multiple years, with initial shipments in 2026 and more meaningful revenue contribution in the second half of that year.

Strong Balance Sheet and Disciplined Capital Allocation

Badger Meter enters this next growth phase with a solid balance sheet. The company reported cash on hand in excess of $225 million, providing ample firepower for both organic investments and acquisitions. In the fourth quarter, management opportunistically repurchased $15 million of shares and marked the 33rd consecutive annual dividend increase. The capital allocation playbook remains consistent: reinvest in the business, pursue strategic M&A, and return excess cash to shareholders, all while maintaining financial flexibility.

Project Pacing Drives Sequential Sales Decline

Despite year‑over‑year growth, the company experienced a 6% sequential decline in Q4 utility water sales compared with Q3 2025. Management attributed this to fewer operating days in the quarter and project pacing factors such as timing and customer/project mix. They cautioned that similar unevenness should be expected into the first half of 2026, with activity and growth skewed toward the second half. This highlights the project‑driven nature of the business, where quarter‑to‑quarter swings may not reflect underlying demand trends.

Timing Uncertainty for Large AMI Deployments

While the PRASA contract is a major win, management was explicit that they will not provide a specific 2026 revenue figure for the project. Large AMI deployments frequently span around five years and can experience shifting schedules that alter quarterly revenue patterns. Badger Meter emphasized that these dynamics introduce timing uncertainty, even when long‑term contract value is solid. For investors, this means focusing more on multi‑year growth rather than near‑term quarterly beats or misses tied to deployment timing.

Commodity and Tariff Headwinds Pressure Margins

Input costs remain a swing factor. Badger Meter reached relative price‑cost parity on tariff impacts in 2025, but management expects tariffs and trade conditions to remain fluid in 2026. Elevated prices for copper and Bi‑alloy ingot components are expected to be a headwind for gross margin. The company set a normalized gross margin guidance range of 39% to 42%, bracketing the strong 42.1% reported in Q4. The message: recent margin strength is sustainable within a range, but not immune to macro cost pressures.

Higher SG&A From Acquisition and Personnel Investments

Selling and administrative expenses (SEA) in Q4 were $49.9 million, up $6.4 million year‑over‑year, largely reflecting the SmartCover acquisition. Excluding SmartCover, base SEA rose a modest 2.9%, mainly due to higher personnel costs. The increase shows both the cost of integrating acquisitions and the company’s willingness to invest in talent to support growth in software, analytics, and large‑scale projects. Management’s focus appears to be on maintaining cost discipline while not starving the business of the resources required to execute its strategy.

Slower Second-Half Base Growth in 2025

Base revenue growth in the second half of 2025 was 6%, below Badger Meter’s long‑term high‑single‑digit growth aspiration. Management attributed the softer pace to concentrated, concluding AMI turnkey projects, which created a tougher comparison and temporary lull as projects wound down. While this slowdown may concern short‑term traders, the company framed it as a timing issue rather than a demand problem, particularly in light of the sizeable PRASA award and ongoing software growth.

Turnkey Projects’ Impact on Margins

Badger Meter reminded investors that turnkey projects—where the company acts as prime contractor and passes through installation and ancillary supplies—tend to carry lower margins. These pass‑through elements can drag on blended margins during heavy turnkey periods even if revenue appears strong. The effect was particularly relevant for concluding AMI turnkey projects in 2025. Over time, the mix between turnkey, supply‑only, and software will remain an important driver of margin volatility.

Manufacturing Location and Cost Structure Considerations

In order to satisfy Buy American requirements for the PRASA contract, Badger Meter is routing production through its Racine facility. Management acknowledged that this may have some impact on margins compared with manufacturing in lower‑cost regions, but emphasized that these factors were built into the project economics when the contract was negotiated. This provides investors some comfort that the company is not chasing headline wins at the expense of profitability.

Guidance and Forward-Looking Outlook

Looking ahead, Badger Meter reaffirmed its long‑term target of high‑single‑digit organic sales growth over the next five years. Management expects 2026 to be back‑loaded, with lower growth in the first half and stronger growth in the second half as project pacing improves and the PRASA deployment ramps. The company guided normalized gross margins to a 39%–42% range, acknowledging that tariffs and higher copper and ingot costs will remain key watchpoints. SmartCover, which generated $40 million in 2025 sales, is fully integrated and on track to be accretive to earnings in 2026. Over the five‑year horizon, Badger Meter anticipates continued operating margin expansion and free cash flow conversion exceeding net earnings, supported by its strong balance sheet and expanding software footprint.

In summary, Badger Meter delivered another year of record revenue, rising margins, and robust cash generation, while reinforcing its evolution toward a more software‑ and analytics‑driven business. Management was transparent about near‑term variability from project timing, cost inflation, and the mix impact of large deployments, but maintained a clear focus on long‑term value creation. For investors willing to look beyond quarterly noise, the company’s combination of structural growth drivers, disciplined capital allocation, and a growing recurring revenue base paints a constructive picture for the years ahead.
